title: Graphardisia subcoriacea
taxonomy: Graphardisia subcoriacea is classified as a species within the family Annonaceae. This family, also known as the custard apple family, is a large and diverse group of flowering plants. Graphardisia subcoriacea is native to Central and South America, where it is commonly found in tropical forests. The plant is a member of the genus Graphardisia, which is a group of flowering plants that are primarily found in the Americas. Graphardisia subcoriacea's classification as a species within the Annonaceae family is a result of its shared characteristics and morphology with other species within this family.

Its leaves and roots are used in traditional medicine to treat a range of ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. intro: Graphardisia subcoriacea, also known as the "yellow sap tree," is a tropical plant species native to Central and South America, belonging to the Annonaceae family. This plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering potential health benefits such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial effects, which can aid in treating various conditions including fever, arthritis, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Graphardisia subcoriacea include alkaloids, terpenoids, and phenolic compounds, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, this plant is often prepared as a tea, infusion, or tincture, and is also used to make topical creams and ointments for its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties.
